misconceptionSummary: Students can sometimes expand by memory but do not understand the bracket as grouped multiplication.

visuals.ruleText: A bracket shows one factor multiplying a whole group
visuals.subText: Expansion reveals what that multiplication looks like term by term

steps.narration: A bracket means the outside factor multiplies the whole group inside. Expanding simply shows that multiplication in full.
This is the distributive idea. Multiplying a sum by two means multiplying each part of the sum by two.
The same idea works in algebra. Four times the whole bracket becomes four times x plus four times one.
If the outside factor is a variable, the same rule still works. X times x gives x squared, and x times two gives two x.

visuals.label: Outside factor multiplies every term inside

steps.narration: So expanding is not a trick. It is a clear way of showing what bracket multiplication really means.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: seeing the bracket as decoration. The bracket groups terms together so the outside factor multiplies all of them.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: The bracket is doing an important job. It tells you the multiplication applies to the whole group inside.
